claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

First, let me read the official guidance to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, do you have any children under the age of 16, or children under 20 who are in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes, I do. I have a child – Alex – who's 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 17 years old, I need to ask:

Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, or an approved training course)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They finished school but haven't gone on to do anything else.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I have a follow-up question:

Did Alex leave education or training within the last 20 weeks, and has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex hasn't registered with the careers service or the armed forces. They just left school and that's it.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have a few more questions to complete my assessment:

Do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. And does Alex live with you in Manchester?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering my questions. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the information you've provided and the official UK government guidance, I need to inform you of my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Unfortunately, you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why: While Child Benefit is normally available for children under 16, it can continue for young people up to age 20 if they remain in approved education or training. Since Alex is 17 and not currently in approved education or training, you don't qualify under that extension.

There is also a provision that allows Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks after a 16 or 17 year old leaves education, but only if they register with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. Since Alex hasn't done this, you wouldn't qualify for that extension either.

**However**, if Alex's situation changes - for example, if Alex enrolls in approved education or training, or registers with a careers service - you may become eligible at that point. If that happens, you should contact the Child Benefit Office to discuss your options.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which is over the standard age limit of 16 for Child Benefit. According to the rules, you can still qualify for Child Benefit for a child aged 16-19 if they stay in approved education or training. However, Alex is not currently in approved education or training. Additionally, while Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, Alex has not registered with either of these services. Therefore, the claimant does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 17-year-old child Alex. While they meet the residence requirement, Alex does not meet the age-related criteria as they are over 16 and not in approved education or training, nor have they registered with a careers service or armed forces within 20 weeks of leaving education. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Additionally, while Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, Alex has not registered with either of these services. Therefore, the claimant does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 17-year-old child Alex. While they meet the residence requirement, Alex does not meet the age-related criteria as they are over 16 and not in approved education or training, nor have they registered with a careers service or armed forces within 20 weeks of leaving education. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
